source_intake_record.raw_question_text: A particle is confined inside a box and can move only along the $x$-axis. The ends of the box move toward the center with a speed small compared with the particle's speed (Fig. 2.75). When the walls are a distance $x$ apart what average external force must be applied to each wall in order to move it at constant speed $V$ ? (UC, Berkeley)

the expected result has clear symbolic structure, making algebraic equivalence checking feasible

candidate_problem_record.subject: 物理
candidate_problem_record.raw_question_text: A particle is confined inside a box and can move only along the $x$-axis. The ends of the box move toward the center with a speed small compared with the particle's speed (Fig. 2.75). When the walls are a distance $x$ apart what average external force must be applied to each wall in order to move it at constant speed $V$ ? (UC, Berkeley)
candidate_problem_record.multi_solution_policy_rationale: 该数据集保留多解潜力评估，但不默认强推多解 agent。

extraction_notes: image field contains a PIL image object representation rather than a file path or URL
metadata.reasoning_chain:
Consider a collision of the particle with one wall. The momentum acquired by the particle is
$$\mathbf{p}+2 m \mathbf{V}-(-\mathbf{p})=2 \mathbf{p}+2 m \mathbf{V}$$
The interval between two consecutive collisions with the wall is
$$T^{\prime}= rac{2 x}{\left( rac{p}{m}ight)}= rac{2 x m}{p}$$
so that the change of momentum due to collisions with the wall in a time $d t$ is
$$d p=2(p+m V)  rac{d t}{T^{\prime}}$$
Hence
$$ rac{d p}{d t}= rac{(p+m V) p}{x m} \approx  rac{p^{2}}{x m}= rac{p_{0}^{2} x_{0}^{2}}{m x^{3}}$$
as $ rac{p}{m} \gg V$. This is the force exerted by the wall on the particle. To keep the walls moving at constant speed, a force of the same magnitude must apply to each wall. The problem can also be solved using the Hamiltonian formalism. Use a reference frame attached to one of the walls, say the left-hand wall. As shown in Fig. 2.75, the particle has velocity $- rac{p}{m}-V$. The Hamiltonian is
$$ egin{aligned}
H & = rac{1}{2} m\left( rac{p}{m}+Vight)^{2} \
& = rac{1}{2 m}(p+m V)^{2} \approx  rac{p^{2}}{2 m}= rac{p_{0}^{2} x_{0}^{2}}{2 m x^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$
The force on the particle is $\dot{p}$ which is given by Hamilton's equation:
$$\dot{p}=- rac{\partial H}{\partial x}= rac{p_{0}^{2} x_{0}^{2}}{m x^{3}}.$$

$$ rac{d p}{d t}= rac{(p+m V) p}{x m} \approx  rac{p^{2}}{x m}= rac{p_{0}^{2} x_{0}^{2}}{m x^{3}}$$

The problem can also be solved using the Hamiltonian formalism. Use a reference frame attached to one of the walls, say the left-hand wall. The force on the particle is $\dot{p}$ which is given by Hamilton's equation:
